rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is not an emergency: afebrile, stable, mild symmetric synovitis, widespread centralized pain. However, telehealth cannot examine the joints or exclude septic arthritis in an immunosuppressed (methotrexate) patient, and the patient is bedbound and dehydrating while living alone. The proportionate action is prompt in-person review (joint exam + CRP/ESR) within a short timeframe — an urgent, but not emergency, tier. T2 (routine GP) is the minimum viable pass; T4/T5 would be over-triage unless a single hot joint or high fever appears.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A single joint becomes intensely red, hot and maximally swollen, or fever spikes above 38.5 C with uncontrollable shivering
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the Emergency Department immediately / call for urgent care
escalation_edges.rationale: These features indicate possible septic arthritis, which in an immunosuppressed patient is a surgical emergency requiring urgent joint aspiration.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If any single joint suddenly becomes bright red, hot and hugely swollen, or you spike a fever over 38.5 with shaking chills, go to the Emergency Department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep fluids down, worsening confusion, or cannot care for self at home while alone
escalation_edges.rationale: Dehydration and an unsafe solo home situation on methotrexate risk renal injury and require timely face-to-face help.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep fluids down or you can't safely manage at home on your own, arrange to be seen the same day or call someone to help you get care.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is currently bedbound
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for arranging in-person review and practical support; check whether someone can bring fluids and help attend the appointment.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Methotrexate immunosuppression on a background of RA
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Maintain a lower threshold to exclude septic arthritis and to check renal function/hydration.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Arrange to be seen in person within the next day or two so your joints can be examined and blood tests done. In the meantime keep taking your usual methotrexate and paracetamol, use heat, pace gently, and keep sipping fluids.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Contact your GP/rheumatology service for a prompt in-person appointment; use ED for the emergency features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If the emergency features appear after hours, use the Emergency Department rather than waiting.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring a list of your current medications and note when this flare started and how your knuckles and morning stiffness have changed.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring the patient to self-manage without any in-person joint assessment, missing a possible septic joint in an immunosuppressed patient.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving septic MCP/knee joint is not aspirated; the joint is destroyed and the patient becomes septic.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending an afebrile, stable centralized-pain flare to ED by ambulance, medicalising a non-emergency and consuming emergency resources.
